participants.extracted: Participants included 30 treatment-seeking adolescent victims of childhood sexual abuse (mean age 14.8 years, SD 1.5, range 13–17) and their caregivers (72.6% biological parents, 17.1% other family members, and 10.3% non-familial guardians). The sample was predominantly female (88%) and racially diverse: 46% African American, 37.5% white, 4.2% Native American, 8.3% biracial, and 4% Hispanic. Most participants were from families receiving Medicaid (70%). Youth were recruited from an urban clinic specializing in trauma treatment; inclusion criteria required ages 12–17, at least one lifetime recollectable incident of childhood sexual abuse, and no mental retardation. Nine participants (30%) reported multiple sexual abuse revictimization experiences, 67.7% had experienced other traumatic events, and the mean time since the most recent assault was 3.7 years (SD 3.8).

interventions.extracted: Participants were randomly assigned to either Risk Reduction through Family Therapy (RRFT) or treatment as usual (TAU). RRFT is a family-based, trauma-focused integrated psychotherapy for adolescent victims of childhood sexual abuse. It combines elements of empirically supported treatments, including Trauma-Focused Cognitive Behavioral Therapy, multisystemic and family therapy principles, psychoeducation, coping skills training, substance use intervention, trauma symptom treatment, healthy dating and sexual decision-making, and revictimization risk reduction. Sessions were delivered weekly for 60–90 minutes with the adolescent and caregiver(s), using a flexible clinical pathways approach tailored to each family’s needs. TAU served as the comparison condition and consisted of usual clinic care, which varied across families and commonly included psychotherapy components such as psychoeducation, coping, safety planning, and cognitive-behavioral interventions, but no single standardized treatment protocol was consistently delivered.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure mental health symptoms, substance use, substance use risk factors, and risky sexual behavior across baseline, post-treatment, 3-month follow-up, and 6-month follow-up. Mental health outcomes included PTSD symptoms assessed with the UCLA PTSD Index for DSM-IV-Adolescent and Caregiver versions (UCLA PTSD-A and UCLA PTSD-P), depression symptoms assessed with the Child Depression Inventory (CDI), and internalizing and externalizing symptoms assessed with the Behavioral Assessment System for Children, Second Edition (BASC-2; parent and youth self-report). Substance use outcomes included days of alcohol/drug use over the past 90 days assessed with the Time Line Follow Back Interview (TLFB), with urine drug screens used for validation. Substance use risk factor outcomes included family cohesion and family conflict measured by the Cohesion and Conflict subscales of the Family Environment Scale (FES), completed by adolescents and caregivers. Risky sexual behavior outcomes included the number of consensual sexual intercourse partners over the past 3 months and whether the youth had been diagnosed with a sexually transmitted disease in the past 3 months.
